title: What Is a Database?
intro: Before you store a single task, you need to understand what a database actually is and isn't. Swipe right on true statements, left on false ones.

teach.title: Why Your App Needs a Database
teach.body: Right now, if a user creates a task and refreshes the page, it's gone. That's because the data only lives in the browser's memory. A database is a structured place to **permanently store and retrieve data**. Think of it as your app's long-term memory — without it, your task manager has amnesia.

teach.title: Filing Cabinet vs. Sticky Notes
teach.body: Storing data without a database is like writing everything on sticky notes — it works until a breeze comes through. A database is like a filing cabinet with labeled folders. Everything has a place, you can find things quickly, and nothing disappears when you close the drawer. For our task manager, the database stores every task, every user, and every due date — reliably and permanently.

teach.title: Your Brain vs. a Notebook
teach.body: Try memorizing 50 phone numbers. You can't — your brain drops them after a while. That's your app without a database. Now write them in a notebook with tabs for each letter. You can find any number in seconds, and they're still there next month. A database is your app's notebook — organized, searchable, and permanent.

No real product exists without a database. The moment you learn to store and retrieve data, you're no longer building toy projects — you're building software that can serve real users and generate real revenue.
A database stores your app's data permanently so it survives page refreshes, server restarts, and user sessions. Without one, your task manager is just a fancy notepad that erases itself.

cards.content: A database stores data permanently so it survives when users close the browser
cards.explanation: Correct! Persistence is the core purpose of a database. Without it, all tasks would vanish on page reload.

cards.content: All databases require you to write SQL queries to store and retrieve data
cards.explanation: Not true. While SQL databases (like PostgreSQL) use SQL, NoSQL databases like MongoDB use different query methods. There are many types of databases with different interfaces.

cards.content: A database lets multiple users access the same data without overwriting each other's work
cards.explanation: Correct! Databases handle concurrent access — so if two users create tasks at the same time, both tasks are saved properly.

cards.content: You can use localStorage in the browser as your app's main database for production
cards.explanation: localStorage is useful for caching, but it's limited to one browser on one device, has no structure enforcement, and can be cleared by the user. It's not a real database.

cards.content: A relational database organizes data into tables with rows and columns, similar to a spreadsheet
cards.explanation: Correct! Relational databases like PostgreSQL (which Supabase uses) store data in structured tables. Each row is a record, each column is a field.

cards.content: Databases don't enforce any rules — you can store any data in any format anywhere
cards.explanation: Wrong. Databases enforce schemas, data types, and constraints. For example, you can require that every task must have a title, or that a due date must be a valid date.

cards.content: Databases can search through millions of records in milliseconds using indexes
cards.explanation: Correct! Indexes are like a book's table of contents — they let the database jump straight to the data you need instead of scanning every row.
